channel.title: Close Protection Officers by City on Close Protection and Security Services | CloseProtectionHire.com
channel.description: Recent content in Close Protection Officers by City on Close Protection and Security Services | CloseProtectionHire.com

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Aarhus, Denmark
item.description: An officer assigned to Aarhus spends most of a detail with the corporate sector rather than the street. Maersk runs logistics and services functions from the city, Vestas anchors Denmark’s wind-turbine industry here, and the Port of Aarhus is the country’s largest container hub, handling over half of Denmark’s container traffic. Arla Foods, Salling Group and Jysk round out a headquarters presence that gives Aarhus a genuine corporate weight most visitors underestimate next to Copenhagen.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Charleroi, Belgium
item.description: Charleroi’s protection work sits astride two very different environments. Gosselies, home to Sonaca’s aerospace-structures operations and the A6K digital-innovation hub backed by European Recovery and Resilience Plan funding, is a modern, access-controlled business campus that officers treat on its own terms. The city centre, postal code 6000, is a different picture: Federal Police-sourced reporting identifies it as accounting for a significant share of the wider police zone’s vehicle-related theft, which is why standard vehicle-security awareness and vetted transport after dark form the baseline for any detail here.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Cordoba, Spain
item.description:
Ask a Cordoba-based officer what actually drives the job, and heat comes up before crime does. AEMET, Spain’s state meteorological agency, recorded the country’s all-time temperature record here: 47.4C at La Rambla on 14 August 2021. An officer running a June-to-September detail treats a verified air-conditioned vehicle and a genuine hydration schedule as core protective planning, not a courtesy.

The other half of the job sits in the Mezquita-Catedral ticket queue and the Juderia’s narrow lanes, Cordoba’s densest tourist corridors and the areas most consistently flagged for pickpocketing. That calls for close, briefed positioning rather than a static perimeter, a discipline that matters even more during the UNESCO-listed Patios Festival and the Feria de Cordoba each May, when old-town crowd density rises sharply. Officers working Cordoba hold the Escolta Privado qualification under Ley 5/2014, distinct from and beyond the base Vigilante de Seguridad guard licence, and deploy unarmed as standard, since Guardia Civil authorisation for armed work stays limited to narrow contexts like cash-in-transit.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Gdansk, Poland
item.description: Gdansk asks its officers to carry more history in mind than most European assignments. The Gdansk Shipyard strike that began on 14 August 1980 led within weeks to the Gdansk Agreement and the formal registration of the Solidarity trade union that November, and the European Solidarity Centre beside the original shipyard gates keeps that history active. Anniversary periods each mid-August can bring elevated official security presence and institutional visits, and officers coordinate any delegation access through the host institution well ahead of travel rather than assuming standard entry.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Hannover, Germany
item.description: Hannover Messe defines most of the protection work here. Deutsche Messe AG’s flagship show is widely regarded within industrial and manufacturing circles as the world’s largest industrial trade fair, drawing more than 4,000 exhibiting companies from over 70 countries in a typical edition, and an officer assigned to fair week plans first around exceptional hotel and transfer scarcity, then coordinates with venue security across one of the largest exhibition grounds anywhere.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Katowice, Poland
item.description: Katowice’s protection work tracks a genuine economic transition. For most of the twentieth century this was Upper Silesia’s coal-mining and heavy-industry capital; today the Katowice Special Economic Zone, established in 1996, has drawn more than PLN 50 billion in investment and over 100,000 jobs, named Europe’s best special economic zone in a 2024 fDi Intelligence assessment. Officers assigned to Katowice increasingly cover financial-services and business-process visitors rather than purely industrial delegations, reflecting a business-services sector that grew nearly 70 percent in four years.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Namur, Belgium
item.description: Namur is the one Walloon assignment built almost entirely around institutional access rather than street-level threat. The city has been Wallonia’s official capital since 1986, confirmed by the Parliament of Wallonia in 2010, and hosts both the Parliament of Wallonia and the Government of Wallonia at the Elysette. Officers covering any delegation here coordinate access through the host institution well in advance, since sitting weeks bring extra security presence and occasional temporary restrictions around the government quarter that a standard corporate visit simply does not encounter.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Nuremberg, Germany
item.description: Nuremberg’s protection work runs on the exhibition calendar as much as on any threat assessment. NürnbergMesse ranks among the world’s largest trade fair venues, and Spielwarenmesse, its flagship show, draws close to 67,000 trade visitors a year. An officer assigned to fair week plans around severe hotel and transfer scarcity first, then coordinates with venue security once on site, since the exhibition centre’s own access protocols shape movement more than any external risk.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Rouen, France
item.description: Rouen’s officers work a split profile few French assignments require in one city. On one side is Vieux Rouen, the medieval core where Joan of Arc was executed in 1431, well-policed but pedestrianised enough that vehicle access needs planning ahead of time. On the other is the Seine valley’s industrial corridor, anchored by the Port of Rouen, Europe’s leading grain-export port under HAROPA PORT, where the September 2019 Lubrizol Seveso-site fire remains the standing reference point for petrochemical risk. Any site visit into that corridor gets coordinated in advance through the host organisation rather than approached as open ground.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Wroclaw, Poland
item.description: Most Wroclaw assignments spend their time far from the postcard old town. The city hosted 234 modern business-service centres as of a May 2025 KPMG Poland report, with named operations from Google, HP, Nokia, IBM, UBS and Volvo IT, and officers here work the business-park corridor, moving between employer sites in a single visit more often than covering tourist itineraries. That said, UBS’s Wroclaw headcount reportedly fell from around 2,200 to between 1,400 and 1,600 by late 2024, a reminder that this corporate footprint moves with global business cycles rather than staying fixed.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Antwerp, Belgium
item.description: Two very different jobs sit inside the same Antwerp assignment. One is discreet, jewellery-quarter work around the Antwerp World Diamond Centre, where the officer’s task is staying close without drawing attention in a district already policed by its own dedicated Antwerp Local Police unit. The other is industrial: the Port of Antwerp-Bruges petrochemical cluster, Europe’s largest integrated chemical complex, where gate-to-gate movement, PPE, and formal site inductions replace the diamond quarter’s quieter choreography.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Bremen, Germany
item.description: An officer assigned to Bremen typically works one of two very different environments. The first is Airbus’s second-largest German production site, a 450,000 square metre facility employing more than 4,000 people, where formal accreditation and defined visitor routes govern every movement. The second is the port complex Bremen shares with Bremerhaven, one of Europe’s largest seaport groupings, roughly 65km north along the A27, which brings its own site-access rules and a genuinely separate logistics problem for anyone covering both locations on the same trip.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Dusseldorf, Germany
item.description:
The same officer covering a Messe Dusseldorf trade fair one week might be briefing an interpreter for a Japanese corporate client the next. Dusseldorf’s international profile, more than fifty fairs a year at Messe Dusseldorf and the largest Japanese business community in Germany, concentrated around Immermannstrasse, means officer coverage here has to flex between event-driven crowd management and the quieter, discreet protection that discreet corporate visits call for.

Every officer working the city holds a Sachkundeprufung certificate through IHK Dusseldorf and is registered under the Bewachungsverordnung, a framework revised with a new examination scoring system from 1 July 2025. Armed authorisation stays rare, so the default is unarmed protection built on route planning rather than a visible security presence. Germany’s terrorism threat assessment has remained elevated since 2016, and FCDO guidance (2026) flags crowded events as part of that picture, a description that applies squarely to Dusseldorf’s trade fair calendar and its Rosenmontag carnival parade, which draws several hundred thousand people through the Altstadt each year.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Leipzig, Germany
item.description: Connewitz sets the tone for how a Leipzig officer plans a route, not because the district poses a routine threat to visitors, but because its documented history of confrontation between demonstrators and police, most recently after the 2023 sentencing of Lina E., means any protest activity there needs checking before movement is finalised. FCDO Germany’s advice to avoid all demonstrations applies with real weight here, since these can form on short notice.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Liege, Belgium
item.description: Liege runs on freight. Liege Airport is the fifth-largest cargo airport in Europe by volume, operating around the clock with no noise curfew, and that scale is what pulls logistics and e-commerce executives into the city in the first place. It also means an officer here spends more time coordinating advance access to cargo terminals than escorting principals through tourist crowds, since LGG itself carries very little scheduled passenger traffic.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Lille, France
item.description: Officers working Lille spend as much time around Eurostar and TGV platforms as they do inside the city itself, a reflection of Lille’s role as the cross-border rail hub linking Paris, Brussels and London. That status is also why France’s highest Vigipirate tier, Urgence Attentat, shapes route planning here more visibly than in many comparably sized French cities: transport hubs sit squarely within the national target profile the posture addresses.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Montpellier, France
item.description: An officer’s day in Montpellier is more likely to involve a Sanofi site visit or a French Tech Montpellier conference than anything resembling a security incident. The city has held French Tech Capital status since 2019, IBM has kept a major presence since 1965, and a genuine biotech cluster around Sanofi draws a steady stream of pharmaceutical and life sciences principals, most of whom need a low-profile, business-dressed officer rather than a visible protective detail.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Palma de Mallorca, Spain
item.description:
A Palma assignment can look completely different depending on the client. One brief is a superyacht owner arriving through Real Club Nautico de Palma or STP Shipyard Palma, where the job is a discreet, well-timed quayside-to-residence handover. The next might be a family group staying at a Son Vida villa, where officers coordinate with the estate’s own private security rather than operating independently in the gated hillside enclave.

Licensing here runs through Spain’s Ley de Seguridad Privada 5/2014, with firms registered through the SES and individual officers carrying a TIP issued by the Direccion General de la Policia. Armed authorisation stays rare for commercial work, so unarmed deployment is the norm. FCDO Spain’s 2024 guidance is specific about the practical risks: organised distraction theft around the cruise terminal and tourist core, drink spiking including GHB in nightlife venues, and documented falls linked to balcony misuse, particularly relevant for younger principals or family groups on a Mallorca visit. Recurring anti-tourism demonstrations since 2024 add a schedule-affecting factor without amounting to a serious threat to visitors who plan around them.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Strasbourg, France
item.description: No other city on this list carries Strasbourg’s documented attack history. On 11 December 2018, a gunman killed five people and wounded eleven at the Christmas Market before being killed by police after a roughly 700-officer manhunt, an event that remains part of every officer’s briefing for any Strasbourg assignment during the Christkindelsmarik period, when the market draws around two million visitors across the Grande Ile. That history, combined with Strasbourg’s role hosting European Parliament plenary sessions, the Council of Europe, and the European Court of Human Rights, gives the city a genuine institutional target profile that shapes how officers plan movement here more than in most other French assignments.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Stuttgart, Germany
item.description: Two of the world’s most recognisable car makers headquarter their production and R&D in Stuttgart, and that alone defines a large share of the officer work here. Mercedes-Benz Group AG in Untertuerkheim and Porsche AG in Zuffenhausen both run formal site accreditation, and officers brief principals on device and meeting security beforehand, not as a formality but because Germany’s domestic intelligence service treats the automotive sector as an active espionage target in its annual reporting.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Bologna, Italy
item.description: An officer working in Bologna is licensed twice over: once personally, through Questura di Bologna credentials and the GPG qualification, and once through the Prefettura registration of the firm that employs them. That distinction matters to a buyer, because a company licence alone says nothing about the individual standing next to the principal. The threat picture officers plan against is modest by international standards, dominated by pickpocketing around Piazza Maggiore and the porticoes rather than anything requiring an armed response, which the Questura in any case grants sparingly.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Bordeaux, France
item.description: Bordeaux asks an officer to move between three quite different environments in the same assignment: the formal reception district around Place de la Bourse and the Miroir d’Eau, the access-controlled aerospace and defence gates at Merignac and Bordeaux-Lac serving Dassault Aviation, Thales, and MBDA, and the rural wine-estate circuit through the Medoc, Pauillac, and Saint-Emilion. Each demands a different register, from formal business dress in the city centre to estate-appropriate planning for a multi-day chateau visit well outside urban infrastructure. Licensing is the same CNAPS carte professionnelle required nationally, checked as an individual credential separate from the employing firm’s own authorisation.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Eindhoven, Netherlands
item.description: Eindhoven is unusual among the cities in this network because the sharpest planning consideration an officer faces here is not street crime, which is low, but a documented espionage risk tied to the local technology sector. The AIVD, the Dutch intelligence service, named ASML and NXP Semiconductors specifically in its 2023 annual report as targets of state-actor corporate espionage, and any officer briefed for an assignment connected to those firms needs to fold counter-surveillance and information-security awareness into an otherwise standard close-protection plan. Licensing itself follows the WPBR framework: Politie integrity screening for the individual, Justis approval for the employing firm, and armed protection is not commercially available in the Netherlands regardless of the client’s profile.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Genoa, Italy
item.description: Genoa presents a different working environment for a close protection officer than the flatter Italian cities: the Caruggi, its dense medieval alley network, has no vehicle access at all, so an officer here plans a foot-escort route from the outset rather than defaulting to a car. Licensing follows the same national pattern as the rest of Italy, with Questura di Genova credentials and the GPG qualification required of the individual, and a separate Prefettura di Genova registration required of the firm. Armed authorisation exists on paper but is rarely used commercially, which keeps the standard posture unarmed and route-planning led.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Ghent, Belgium
item.description: Ghent puts its most attractive hospitality district and its most documented crime hotspot in the same place: Federale Politie data identifies the Graslei and Korenmarkt historic waterfront as a concentration point for opportunistic crime, and that is also where most principal hotel and dining engagements in the city happen. An officer working Ghent treats that overlap as routine, not a contradiction, keeping alertness elevated in the waterfront district precisely because it is also the venue of choice. The Overpoort student nightlife area carries a similar profile after dark. Licensing follows Belgium’s 2017 private-security law: a Ministry of Interior badge for the individual officer, an SPF Interieur agrement for the employing firm, with armed authorisation rarely issued for corporate work.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Malaga, Spain
item.description: Malaga’s close protection market splits neatly into two worlds that an officer has to move between. One is the Tarjeta de Identidad Profesional, the individual credential issued by the Direccion General de la Policia under Ley de Seguridad Privada 5/2014, without which no escolta privado can legally work; the firm employing that officer needs its own, separate Secretaria de Estado de Seguridad registration. The other is the split operational profile the city demands: formal business dress for TechPark meetings involving firms like Vodafone, Google, and Amazon’s regional offices, against a deliberately lower-signature presence for the Costa del Sol resort corridor toward Marbella.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Nantes, France
item.description: An officer working Nantes needs to hold two very different assignments in mind at once: hospitality and reception work around Place du Commerce and Cours des 50-Otages, which is also one of the city’s recurring protest-assembly points, and industrial-site coverage that can mean a 60km run west to the Chantiers de l’Atlantique shipyard in Saint-Nazaire. Licensing is the standard French model, a CNAPS carte professionnelle held by the individual and a separate CNAPS authorisation held by the employing firm, with armed work rarely approved for corporate bookings either way.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Rennes, France
item.description: An officer working in Rennes carries a CNAPS carte professionnelle as a personal credential, separate from whatever authorisation the employing firm holds, and a client checking only the company paperwork is missing half the picture. France sits nationally at Vigipirate Urgence Attentat, which raises the baseline security presence at transport hubs, but Rennes itself has no city-specific elevation above that. The factor that actually shapes daily planning here is more local: Place de la Republique and Place du Parlement, the heart of the hotel and meeting district, are also where Rennes’ recurring demonstrations over pension reform and agricultural policy tend to assemble.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Utrecht, Netherlands
item.description: Utrecht Centraal is the busiest railway station in the Netherlands, and for an officer working this city that fact matters more than almost anything else: the station and its adjoining Hoog Catharijne shopping centre are the one part of Utrecht where pickpocketing is a recognised, recurring issue, and route planning through the complex gets treated differently from a walk along the quieter Oudegracht canal belt. Licensing follows the standard Dutch WPBR model, Politie integrity screening for the individual officer and Justis approval for the employing firm, with armed protection not commercially available regardless of the assignment.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Zaragoza, Spain
item.description: In Zaragoza, the real point of arrival for most principals is not the airport but Zaragoza Delicias station: the AVE high-speed line puts Madrid 80 minutes away and Barcelona 90, and an officer here builds the collection plan around the concourse rather than the smaller Zaragoza Airport, 9km out. Licensing follows Spain’s national pattern, with the individual officer holding a Tarjeta de Identidad Profesional from the Direccion General de la Policia and the employing firm separately registered with the Secretaria de Estado de Seguridad under Ley de Seguridad Privada 5/2014.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Bilbao, Spain
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Bilbao: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Bilbao network holds a valid Tarjeta de Identidad Profesional, and is assessed for local competence in the Basque political context. It covers standard team configuration, the specific authorisation required for the uncommon armed option, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Bridgetown, Barbados
item.description:
This page is about the people who provide close protection in Bridgetown: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Bridgetown network holds SIA-Barbados licensing plus Royal Barbados Police Force authorisation where armed, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

The island’s compact size is simultaneously an advantage and a constraint. Advantage: the entire island is covered by strong communications infrastructure, emergency services response times are relatively short, and the main hospital and private medical facilities are within 30 minutes of any point on the island. Constraint: the limited road network means that route variation is genuinely difficult, and a determined surveillance team with knowledge of the principal’s accommodation and schedule could profile transfer movements without particular effort. The practical mitigations are time variation, use of the private charter terminal at Grantley Adams where possible, and avoidance of predictable restaurant and leisure venue choices during extended stays. These are standard CPO disciplines that apply here with more operational weight than in a city with hundreds of possible routes.

item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Dammam: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Dammam network operates under Saudi Council of Ministers Resolution No. 58 licensing, and is assessed for Eastern Province-specific competence. It covers standard team configuration, the state-controlled basis for armed elements, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Maracaibo, Venezuela
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Maracaibo: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Maracaibo network is a long-established, verifiable provider operating within MPPRIJP regulatory bounds, given inconsistent enforcement, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration and the extra due-diligence a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Medina, Saudi Arabia
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Medina: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Medina network is licensed under the national Saudi framework via Council of Ministers Resolution No. 58, and is assessed for local competence including access-restriction compliance. It covers standard team configuration, the requirement that all personnel in restricted areas be Muslim, and the checks a buyer should insist on.

item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Nassau: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. It covers standard team configuration and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Nice: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Nice network holds current CNAPS licensing, and is assessed for local competence including terrorism-history awareness and HNWI-protection experience. It covers standard team configuration, cross-border Monaco jurisdictional awareness, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in The Hague: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our The Hague network is registered with Justis under the WPBR and screened to a standard appropriate for the city’s international-institution environment, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, diplomatic-protection coordination, and the checks a buyer should insist on.

item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Thessaloniki: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. item.title: Close Protection Officers in Astana, Kazakhstan
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Astana: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Astana network holds mandatory CCSP licensing under Kazakhstan’s Ministry of Internal Affairs, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, the Kazakh-nationals-only rule for armed personnel, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Barranquilla
item.description:
This page is about the people who provide close protection in Barranquilla: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Barranquilla network holds SVSP credentials, with military authorisation for firearms where armed, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

For the full Barranquilla security picture, see the Barranquilla city briefing for Policia Nacional Colombia advisory context. For vetted airport transfers as part of a CPO detail, secure airport transfers in Barranquilla covers the Ernesto Cortissoz Airport collection protocol and Circunvalar route protocols.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Basra, Iraq
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Basra: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Basra network holds PSCD licensing under Law No. 2 of 2017, held by both company and individual, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, energy-sector-specific experience, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Bern, Switzerland
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Bern: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Bern network is licensed under Canton Bern’s private security act alongside the inter-cantonal Concordat, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, the almost-exclusively-unarmed model, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Brisbane
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Brisbane: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Brisbane network holds a current QPS licence under the Security Providers Act 1993, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, the specific Weapons Licensing Branch authority required for armed roles, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Busan

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Fortaleza
item.description:
This page is about the people who provide close protection in Fortaleza: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Fortaleza network holds SINARM and Policia Federal registration plus SSPDS-CE state credentials, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

Against this commercial backdrop sits a security environment that consistently places Fortaleza among Brazil’s most challenging urban centres. The Brazilian Public Security Forum and IPEA have documented elevated homicide rates in Fortaleza across multiple reporting years, driven substantially by criminal faction conflict within the state. FCDO Brazil travel advice highlights the importance of heightened vigilance in Fortaleza and recommends against complacency even in hotel zones. US OSAC Brazil reporting is similarly specific about the city’s elevated risk profile.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Havana, Cuba
item.description:
This page is about how close protection support actually works in Havana: there is no independent private security licensing framework, and all security functions are ultimately controlled by MININT and the Revolutionary Armed Forces. It covers the local liaison model used instead, how joint-venture hotel operations provide a security layer, and the extra due-diligence and communications-security preparation applied given the absence of a conventional CPO market.

The sectors that drive international business visits to Havana include the tourism and hospitality industry (Cuba’s primary foreign-exchange earner, attracting cruise industry and hotel investment discussions), the energy sector through CUPET state oil company partnerships, BioCubaFarma’s internationally recognised pharmaceutical and vaccine research programmes, and diplomatic engagements that draw on Cuba’s neutral and non-aligned positioning for bilateral meetings. As US sanctions policy evolves, the landscape of commercially permissible activity for US-adjacent businesses and their counterparts changes periodically, making legal review alongside security planning an essential pre-travel step. The economic crisis that has deepened since 2020, including widespread shortages, power outages, and the erosion of purchasing power for Cuban citizens, has created a social environment in which the visible affluence of foreign visitors is more salient than it was previously, with documented consequences for petty crime and harassment in the tourist zones (FCDO Cuba, 2024).

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Ibadan, Nigeria
item.description:
This page is about the people who provide close protection in Ibadan: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Ibadan network holds NSCDC registration under the NSCDC Act 2003, with Oyo State coordination, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

The security environment in Ibadan is high by West African standards, and requires the same clear-eyed risk assessment that Nigeria demands across most of its geography. FCDO Nigeria (2024) identifies armed robbery – including carjacking and vehicle robbery at junctions – as a significant documented risk in Ibadan. All night movement is classified as extremely high-risk and should be treated as operationally prohibited rather than merely discouraged. Kidnapping of foreign nationals is flagged explicitly by FCDO Nigeria (2024) as a risk across Nigeria including Oyo State, and anti-kidnap protocols are a non-negotiable component of any CPO programme for this city. The Lagos-Ibadan Expressway – the primary road link from the international gateway at Lagos – is documented by the Federal Road Safety Corps of Nigeria as one of the country’s most accident-prone highways, and the transfer management from LOS to Ibadan is one of the most operationally significant elements of the entire programme. These risks are manageable with a properly designed and properly staffed protection programme, but they cannot be managed with a casual or improvised approach.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Krakow, Poland
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Krakow: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. item.title: Close Protection Officers in Kumasi, Ghana
item.description:
This page is about the people who provide close protection in Kumasi: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Kumasi network is registered under Ghana’s Private Security Organisation Act 1993, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, individual armed-personnel licensing, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

The risk environment is moderate by West African standards, with the primary security concerns for visiting executives falling into two distinct categories: urban petty crime in Kumasi’s dense commercial areas, and road safety risk on the highway routes connecting the city to its mining hinterland. FCDO Ghana (2024) identifies Kejetia Market and Asafo Market – two of West Africa’s largest and most intensely trafficked commercial environments – as the primary zones for pickpocketing and bag-snatching targeting foreign visitors. The N6 highway to Accra and the roads south to Obuasi are documented high-risk routes in WHO road safety reporting, with commercial vehicle accidents and inadequate road surfaces creating hazards that require active management rather than routine vehicle provision. Most international principals arrive via Kotoka International Airport in Accra (ACC) and either connect to Kumasi by domestic flight or by road, and the choice and management of this first transfer is one of the most important security decisions of the Kumasi programme.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Mandalay
item.description:
This page is about who can realistically support a Mandalay security requirement under post-coup conditions: every operator in our Mandalay network holds current Myanmar Police Force licensing and demonstrated post-2021 operational experience, given the unpredictable regulatory environment. It covers the checkpoint-management and evacuation-readiness competence required, and the extra due-diligence a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer in an active civil-war environment.

FCDO advises against all but essential travel to Myanmar. For organisations with personnel deployments in Mandalay, this advice is the baseline from which all planning must start. “Essential” in this context means operations that cannot be conducted remotely or relocated, that carry a documented business or humanitarian rationale, and that have been subject to a formal organisational risk acceptance process. The duty-of-care obligations of employers under the Corporate Manslaughter and Corporate Homicide Act 2007 and related legislation mean that sending staff to a critical-risk environment without appropriate security provision and oversight creates significant legal exposure for the directing organisation.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Nanjing
item.description:

It covers standard support configuration and the checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any provider.

The city’s physical security environment is low risk, consistent with China’s major eastern cities where police presence in commercial zones is extensive and violent crime against foreign visitors is rare. The risk calculus for Nanjing is driven instead by China’s systemic legal and regulatory framework: the National Security Law, the Data Security Law (2021), and the amended Counter-Espionage Law (2023) collectively create an environment where business activities that would be entirely routine in European or North American markets carry legal risk in China. FCDO and US OSAC China country advisories both document these risks and advise business travellers to take them seriously.

China’s physical security environment for international business visitors in cities like Wuhan is low to moderate in terms of conventional crime risks. The risk profile that matters most is legal and regulatory: the National Security Law, the Data Security Law, the Counter-Espionage Law, and the exit ban administrative mechanism create an operating environment with no close parallel in Western markets. FCDO and US OSAC China advisories both note that these legal instruments have been applied to foreign nationals in ways that surprised the individuals and their organisations, and that the risk is not confined to companies in inherently sensitive sectors.

The Vietnam licensing framework

Hanoi’s close protection sector operates under Private Security Decree 96/2016/ND-CP, Ministry of Public Security, administered by Ministry of Public Security, Vietnam. Companies providing close protection services must hold a company licence; individual CPOs must hold a current personal protection endorsement. Both levels require formal training, background vetting, and renewal cycles.

It covers standard team configuration, the restriction on foreign nationals carrying weapons, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

The CPO environment in Antananarivo

Madagascar’s security environment is shaped by recurrent political instability, an elevated petty crime and vehicle-targeting risk profile, and the specific vulnerabilities of the mining sector in a country with remote and difficult terrain. FCDO advises a high degree of caution in Madagascar, reflecting the combination of crime risk in Antananarivo and road safety concerns across the country. The US State Department rates Madagascar at Level 2 (Exercise Increased Caution). The international zone around Isoraka, Ivandry, and Ankorondrano is materially more stable than the outer districts, but the TNR transfer corridor and night movement remain the primary operational risk windows.

The CPO environment in Maseru

Lesotho’s security environment is shaped by periodic political instability, an elevated robbery and vehicle crime risk in Maseru’s outer districts, and the specific operational challenges of the Maluti Mountains for Letseng mine site visits. FCDO advises a heightened degree of caution in Lesotho. The US State Department rates Lesotho at Level 2 (Exercise Increased Caution). The diamond sector (Gem Diamonds Letseng), development finance community (World Bank, AfDB, LHWP bilateral donors), and textile sector form the primary international visitor categories. Source: FCDO Lesotho travel advice, 2026; US State Dept Lesotho Level 2 advisory, 2026.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Ouagadougou
item.description:
There is no routine CPO deployment offered in Ouagadougou under current conditions: the formal licensing framework is functioning in a degraded form, and the FCDO/US State Department rate Burkina Faso at the highest risk category. This page covers what specialist Sahel conflict-environment operators actually provide, why conventional CPO placement is not offered, and the extreme due-diligence standard applied to any case-by-case essential-purpose engagement.

The CPO environment in Ouagadougou

Burkina Faso’s security environment deteriorated sharply from 2019 onwards following the expansion of Sahel jihadist groups from Mali. The 2022 military coups accelerated the collapse of civilian security structures and the displacement of international security partners. The MPSR junta under Captain Ibrahim Traore has expelled French forces and sought Wagner Group (now Africa Corps) support, fundamentally altering the security architecture. JNIM and IS Sahel Province have conducted complex attacks in Ouagadougou’s commercial areas and hotels used by international visitors. CPO deployment in this environment requires specialist Sahel expertise, current intelligence, and pre-confirmed extraction planning – not standard corporate close protection. Source: FCDO Burkina Faso travel advice, 2026; US State Dept Burkina Faso Level 4 advisory, 2026.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Asmara
item.description:
This page is about how close protection support actually works in Asmara: there is no independent private security sector, and armed security for foreign visitors is not available through commercial operators. It covers the discreet, low-profile, unarmed service model used instead, the counter-surveillance and behaviour-protocol training operators require, and the extra due-diligence applied given Eritrea’s closed operating environment.

The CPO environment in Asmara

Eritrea is one of the most closed states in the world. No independent media, opposition parties, or civil society exist; freedom of movement is tightly controlled for both nationals and foreigners; and government surveillance of international visitors is pervasive. These conditions shape CPO operations in a fundamentally different way than in standard high-risk environments: the aim is not to be seen as providing close protection, but to provide it invisibly within government-compliant parameters. CPO value in Asmara lies in advance planning, communications security, and the professional discipline to operate without triggering government attention.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Bangui
item.description:
This page is about who can realistically support a Bangui security requirement: the CAR’s regulatory framework has been significantly disrupted by ongoing conflict, so operators must be selected for Central Africa conflict-environment experience rather than formal licensing alone. It covers the case-by-case assessment process for essential humanitarian, diplomatic, or corporate engagements, and the extreme due-diligence standard applied given the active-conflict operating environment.

The CPO environment in Bangui

The Central African Republic has faced persistent armed conflict for over a decade. Multiple armed groups operate across the country, the UN MINUSCA peacekeeping mission maintains a stabilising presence in Bangui, and Africa Corps (formerly Wagner Group) has been embedded as a security partner to the CAR government since the withdrawal of French Sangaris forces in 2022. The capital itself is the most stable location in the country, but PK5 and the outer districts carry specific hazards for international visitors. All CPO operations in Bangui are built around the smallest viable operational footprint: minimum personnel, minimum dwell time, pre-arranged extraction, and MINUSCA coordination at every stage.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Khartoum
item.description:
This page is about who can realistically support a Sudan-linked security requirement under current conditions: formal private security regulation is not functioning in Khartoum given the civil war between SAF and RSF forces. It covers what remote and Port Sudan-based support looks like, why conventional CPO deployment is not offered inside the active conflict zone, and the extreme due-diligence standard applied to any specialist conflict-environment partner we work with.

The Sudan CPO environment in 2026

The conflict that began in April 2023 between the Sudanese Armed Forces and the Rapid Support Forces has devastated Khartoum’s infrastructure and caused mass civilian displacement. All major diplomatic missions evacuated from the capital; the British Embassy, US Embassy, and European missions have operated from Port Sudan or regional monitoring hubs since mid-2023. Khartoum International Airport sustained conflict damage and has remained closed. UNHCR estimated over 8 million people internally displaced in Sudan as of early 2026. Source: FCDO Sudan travel advice, 2026; US State Dept Sudan Level 4 advisory, 2026; UNHCR Sudan situation report, 2026.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Port-au-Prince
item.description:
There is no functioning national private security regulatory authority operating consistently in Port-au-Prince as of 2026. This page covers who can realistically support a Haiti-linked requirement under these conditions: operators must be selected through international security firms with verified Haiti experience, given the collapse of any reliable licensing body, and it covers the extreme due-diligence standard applied given the security vacuum and ongoing gang conflict.

Port-au-Prince CPO: the framework for essential visits

The FCDO advises against all travel to Haiti. The US State Department classifies Haiti at Level 4 (Do Not Travel). These advisories reflect the ground reality. CPO engagement in Port-au-Prince is therefore for essential visits only: UN operations, senior NGO leadership, diplomatic principal movement, and assessed essential business visits. The CPO team is structured as a professional security operation, not a driver-plus-escort arrangement. Source: FCDO Haiti travel advice (2026); US State Dept Haiti Level 4 advisory (2026).

Cameroon’s Ministere de l’Administration Territoriale (MINAT) administers private security licensing in Yaounde. Company and individual licensing verification is the required pre-engagement step. item.title: Close Protection Officers in Kabul
item.description:
This page is about who can realistically support an Afghanistan-linked security requirement under current conditions: there is no functioning civil private security licensing framework, and any Afghan security company still operating does so under Taliban arrangements. It covers how those relationships are vetted, why conventional CPO deployment is not offered without rebuilt post-2021 authorisation, and the extreme due-diligence standard applied to any specialist partner we work with.

The operating model: compound-based, Afghan-led

Western CPO firms largely withdrew from Afghanistan after the 2021 evacuation. Effective CPO operations in Kabul depend entirely on vetted Afghan operators with post-2021 operational experience, Taliban relationship management capability, and proven performance in the IS-KP threat environment. The compound-based operating model minimises external exposure: movements outside secured compounds are treated as high-risk events, planned individually, and conducted with IS-KP target pattern intelligence informing every routing decision.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Mogadishu
item.description:
This page is about the people who provide close protection in Mogadishu: who they are, how they are vetted, and what to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Mogadishu network can demonstrate FGS licensing and documented Mogadishu operational experience, given that national regulatory oversight remains limited, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, compound-security capability, and the checks a buyer should insist on.

Al-Shabaab IED attacks, VBIEDs, complex attacks on fortified facilities, and targeted killings define the threat environment that CPO operations in Mogadishu must address. Armoured vehicles, continuous QRF availability, route-intelligence-led movement planning, and medevac to Nairobi at approximately two hours are the operational baselines from which every assignment is built. Trained CPO professionals operating within the NISA and SPF framework can reduce risk materially for international organisation staff, diplomatic personnel, and essential-travel corporate principals throughout their Mogadishu programme. For principals whose programme extends to other Somali cities or regional hubs, close protection officers in Nairobi covers the standard regional coordination base.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Naypyidaw
item.description:
This page is about who can realistically support a Naypyidaw security requirement in Myanmar’s most controlled city: every operator in our Naypyidaw network holds current licensing and demonstrated experience operating under the Tatmadaw’s direct presence, and is assessed for competence in checkpoint management and detention-risk mitigation. It covers the government-liaison-specific due-diligence and evacuation-readiness capability a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

CPO operations in Naypyidaw are structured entirely around SAC-approved movement parameters, with all principal movements pre-coordinated with the assigned SAC security liaison. Independent route advances and off-plan movements are not operationally possible in this environment; our teams provide close protection within the approved framework, supplementing rather than replacing the official SAC security presence. For principals who also require coverage in Yangon as part of a Myanmar programme, close protection officers in Yangon covers the separate operational environment of the former commercial capital.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Niamey
item.description:
This page is about the people who can realistically support a Niamey requirement post-coup: who they are, and what to verify before engaging them. Every operator in our Niamey network has long-standing local presence and current relationships within Niger’s CNSP-governed environment, since the formal licensing framework is in transition, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration and the extra due-diligence a buyer should insist on.

The FCDO Niger advisory (2025) advises against all but essential travel and identifies terrorism as the primary risk for international visitors. Venue pre-advances, CNSP checkpoint compliance, and rigorous medevac planning to Dakar are the operational pillars of every Niamey assignment. Trained CPO professionals operating within the DSP-licensed framework can meaningfully reduce risk for development sector staff, energy sector executives, and UN-system principals throughout their Niamey programme. For principals requiring integrated secure ground transport alongside CPO cover, security drivers in Niamey coordinate directly with the close protection team from NIM arrival through to departure.

The CPO environment in Medellin

Medellin’s transformation since the 1990s has produced a sophisticated corporate security market. item.description:

It covers sanctions-compliance screening as a mandatory step, compound-based operational competence, and the extra due-diligence a buyer should insist on given the ambiguous legal environment for security operations.

The CPO environment in Yangon

Yangon remains comparatively stable relative to Myanmar’s conflict zones but FCDO and the US State Department’s highest-level warnings apply to the entire country, including Yangon. CPO operations in this environment require operators who understand the post-coup political landscape, checkpoint compliance requirements, and the specific legal risks that make communications discipline and movement management substantively different from lower-risk Southeast Asian cities.
